The Orbit & Ocular Cranial Nerves

42A · Cranial Bones 42B · Contents & CN Course 42C · Clinical Deficits 📐 Figs 7.47–7.59 📝 15 MCQs
LO 42A

The 7 Cranial Bones of the Orbit

Describe and demonstrate the bones contributing to each orbital wall — roof, floor, medial, lateral, and apex.

🧠 Mnemonic
"Friendly Mexicans Zestfully Eat Large Spicy Peppers"
Frontal  ·  Maxilla  ·  Zygoma  ·  Ethmoid  ·  Lacrimal  ·  Sphenoid  ·  Palatine

Orbital Wall Map

The orbit is a pyramidal cavity; base faces anterolaterally, apex points posteromedially toward the optic canal. Think of it as a 4-sided pyramid with the globe inside.

Roof (Superior)
Frontal bone (orbital plate) + Lesser wing of sphenoid
Above: anterior cranial fossa & frontal sinus
Lateral ⚡ Thickest
Zygomatic + Greater wing of sphenoid
Most protected wall
Globe
Medial 🥚 Thinnest
Ethmoid (lamina papyracea) + Lacrimal + Frontal + Sphenoid body
Relates to ethmoid air cells
Floor (Inferior) — Blowout fracture site
Maxilla (orbital surface) + Zygomatic + Palatine
Below: maxillary sinus
FIGURE 7.47Coronal section of the skull — anterior view
FIGURE 7.48Walls of the right orbit — anterior view

The 7 Bones — Detail

Frontal Bone
● Roof · Medial (small)
  • Orbital plate forms the entire roof
  • Trochlear fovea: attachment for SO pulley
  • Lacrimal fossa (lateral roof): lacrimal gland
  • Relates superiorly to anterior cranial fossa
Maxilla
● Floor (major) · Medial (small)
  • Orbital surface = most of the floor
  • Below floor: maxillary sinus → blowout fracture site
  • Infraorbital groove/canal: infraorbital nerve (V2)
  • Inferior orbital fissure (with sphenoid)
Zygoma
● Lateral (anterior) · Floor (anterolateral)
  • Forms anterior portion of lateral wall
  • Zygomaticofacial & zygomaticotemporal foramina
  • "Cheekbone" — easily palpated clinically
Ethmoid
● Medial wall (major contributor)
  • Lamina papyracea = paper-thin orbital plate
  • Thinnest wall → relates to ethmoid air cells
  • Infection: sinuses → orbit (orbital cellulitis)
  • Anterior & posterior ethmoidal foramina
Lacrimal
● Medial wall (anterior)
  • Smallest facial bone
  • Lacrimal groove (with maxilla) → lacrimal fossa → lacrimal sac
  • Nasolacrimal canal → inferior nasal meatus
Sphenoid
● Lateral · Roof · Apex
  • Greater wing: posterior lateral wall
  • Lesser wing: posterior roof; forms apex
  • Optic canal (lesser wing): CN II + ophthalmic artery
  • Superior orbital fissure (greater/lesser wings): CN III, IV, VI, V1
  • Inferior orbital fissure (greater wing + maxilla): V2
Palatine
● Floor (small posterior wedge)
  • Orbital process = smallest contribution of the 7
  • Posterior orbital floor, lateral to palatine canal
  • Often overlooked but anatomically distinct

Key Foramina & Fissures

OpeningBone(s)TransmitsClinical
Optic CanalLesser wing of sphenoidCN II + Ophthalmic arteryFracture → optic neuropathy, vision loss
Superior Orbital FissureBetween greater & lesser wings of sphenoidCN III, IV, VI; V1 branches; superior ophthalmic vein; sympatheticsSOF syndrome = ophthalmoplegia + V1 anesthesia; add optic involvement = orbital apex syndrome
Inferior Orbital FissureSphenoid + MaxillaV2 (infraorbital n.), inferior ophthalmic vein, zygomatic nerveBlowout fracture can entrap structures
Ant. Ethmoidal ForamenEthmoid/FrontalAnterior ethmoidal nerve & arteryEthmoidal arteries: epistaxis
Nasolacrimal CanalLacrimal + MaxillaNasolacrimal duct → inferior meatusDacryostenosis → epiphora (tearing)
🔥 Exam Tip
Medial wall = thinnest (lamina papyracea of ethmoid). Lateral wall = thickest. The floor is intermediate but the classic blowout fracture site — floor collapses into maxillary sinus, entrapping inferior rectus/oblique → vertical diplopia on upward gaze.
LO 42B

Contents of the Orbit & CN Courses

Trace CN III, IV, VI, and V1 from brainstem to orbital target.

Orbital Contents Overview

StructureNotes
Globe (Eyeball)Anterior orbit; optic nerve exits posteriorly through optic canal
Optic Nerve (CN II)Center of annulus of Zinn → optic canal → optic chiasm
7 Extraocular Muscles4 recti (SR, IR, MR, LR) + 2 obliques (SO, IO) + levator palpebrae superioris
Annulus of ZinnFibrous ring at orbital apex; origin of 4 recti. CN II, III (both divisions), VI, nasociliary (V1) pass THROUGH; CN IV + frontal & lacrimal (V1) pass ABOVE
Lacrimal GlandAnterolateral orbit; parasympathetic secretomotor from CN VII via lacrimal nerve (V1 carrier)
Ciliary GanglionParasympathetic relay in posterior orbit, lateral to optic nerve; receives pre-ganglionic from CN III inferior division → short ciliary nerves → sphincter pupillae + ciliary muscle
Ophthalmic ArteryFirst branch of ICA; enters via optic canal; gives: central retinal artery, posterior ciliary, ethmoidal arteries
Ophthalmic VeinsSuperior & inferior; NO VALVES → face infections can spread to cavernous sinus
Orbital FatCushions globe; in Graves' disease, fat + muscle expansion → exophthalmos
FIGURE 7.50Sagittal section through the orbit
FIGURE 7.53Superior approach to right orbit
FIGURE 7.54Superficial dissection of right orbit
FIGURE 7.55Branches of ophthalmic artery
FIGURE 7.56Deep dissection of right orbit
FIGURE 7.57Transection of extraocular muscles
FIGURE 7.58Left orbit with eye removed
Shared Cavernous Sinus Pathway
All 4 nerves traverse the cavernous sinus before the orbit. Within the sinus: CN III, IV, V1 in the lateral wall (superior → inferior); CN VI runs medially, draped directly over the internal carotid. All exit via the superior orbital fissure.

The Four Cranial Nerves

III
Oculomotor Nerve
GSE (motor to 5 muscles) · GVE (parasympathetic)

Origin

Oculomotor nucleus, midbrain tegmentum (superior colliculus level) + Edinger-Westphal nucleus (GVE)

Course

Interpeduncular fossa→ Subarachnoid space(between PCA & SCA)
→Cavernous sinus lateral wall (superior)→SOF → Annulus of Zinn
→Superior division: LPS + SR ·Inferior division: MR + IR + IO

GSE: 5 Muscles

  • Levator palpebrae superioris — elevates eyelid
  • Superior rectus — elevates (when abducted)
  • Medial rectus — adduction
  • Inferior rectus — depresses (when abducted)
  • Inferior oblique — elevates when adducted; extorsion

GVE: Parasympathetic

  • Pre-ganglionic: Edinger-Westphal → rides outer surface of CN III
  • Relay: Ciliary ganglion (posterior orbit, lateral to CN II)
  • Post-ganglionic: Short ciliary nerves
  • Targets: Sphincter pupillae (miosis) + Ciliary muscle (accommodation)
⚠️ Pupil Fiber Location Matters
Parasympathetics ride the outside of CN III. Aneurysm (PComm) compresses outside first → blown pupil before motor loss. Ischemia (DM) hits core first → motor deficit + pupil sparing.
IV
Trochlear Nerve
GSE only · Smallest CN · Only dorsal exit

Origin

Trochlear nucleus, midbrain (inferior colliculus level); fibers decussate internally → exit DORSALLY from posterior midbrain (unique among all CNs)

Course

Posterior midbrain (below inf. colliculus)→wraps around brainstem
→Pierces edge of tentorium cerebelli→Cavernous sinus lateral wall (middle)
→SOF → ABOVE annulus of Zinn→Superior oblique

Muscle Innervated

  • Superior oblique — only target (SO₄)
  • Primary action: intorsion; also depresses when adducted
  • Passes through the trochlea (pulley, medial roof) to reach globe

Unique Features

  • Only CN to exit dorsally from brainstem
  • Longest intracranial course of all CNs → vulnerable to minor trauma
  • Crosses: innervates contralateral superior oblique
  • Passes above annulus (vs III, VI through it)
VI
Abducens Nerve
GSE only · Lateral rectus · Controls conjugate gaze via MLF

Origin

Abducens nucleus in pons, at pontomedullary junction; exits ventral brainstem near midline

Course

Pontomedullary junction→Subarachnoid space
→Under petroclinoid ligament→between dural layers, climbs clivus
→Cavernous sinus (medial, ON internal carotid)→SOF → through annulus → lateral rectus

Muscle Innervated

  • Lateral rectus — only target (LR₆)
  • Function: abduction (lateral movement)

MLF & Conjugate Gaze

  • CN VI nucleus → MLF → contralateral CN III nucleus (medial rectus)
  • CN VI nucleus drives both ipsilateral LR and contralateral MR simultaneously
  • Volitional horizontal gaze requires CN VI to activate CN III
False Localizing Sign
CN VI has the longest intracranial course after CN IV. Elevated ICP stretches it over the clivus → bilateral CN VI palsy = non-localizing sign of raised ICP.
V1
Ophthalmic Division (V)
GSA only · Purely sensory · Smallest trigeminal division

Origin

Trigeminal (Gasserian) ganglion in Meckel's cave; V1 is smallest and most superior division

Course

Trigeminal ganglion→Cavernous sinus lateral wall (inferior)
→SOF → 3 branches

3 Branches in Orbit

  • Frontal nerve (largest; ABOVE annulus): → supraorbital + supratrochlear → forehead, upper eyelid
  • Lacrimal nerve (lateral; ABOVE annulus): lacrimal gland (carries CN VII post-ganglionic parasympathetics), lateral conjunctiva
  • Nasociliary nerve (medial; THROUGH annulus): long ciliary nn. (cornea), ethmoidal nn. (sinuses), external nasal n. (nose tip)

Sensory Territory

  • Cornea & conjunctiva (corneal reflex afferent)
  • Forehead and scalp (anterior to vertex)
  • Upper eyelid, medial nose, nasal dorsum/tip
  • Frontal, ethmoid, sphenoid sinuses

Also Carries

  • Proprioceptive afferents from extraocular muscle spindles → V1 → trigeminal nucleus → oculomotor nuclei (motor feedback loop)

Cavernous Sinus — Nerve Positions

PositionStructureNotes
Lateral wall, superiorCN IIIFirst affected by lateral wall lesion
Lateral wall, middleCN IV
Lateral wall, inferiorV1V2 just below sinus — characteristically spared in cavernous sinus syndrome
Medial (on ICA)CN VIDirectly contacts internal carotid → ICA aneurysm hits CN VI early
CentralInternal carotid artery + sympathetic plexus

Annulus of Zinn — Who Goes Where

Through the annulus

  • ✅ CN III (superior & inferior divisions)
  • ✅ CN VI (abducens)
  • ✅ Nasociliary nerve (V1)
  • ✅ CN II (optic canal is at ring center)

Above/outside the annulus

  • ⬆️ CN IV (trochlear)
  • ⬆️ Frontal nerve (V1)
  • ⬆️ Lacrimal nerve (V1)
  • ⬆️ Superior ophthalmic vein
LO 42C

Clinical Deficits from Orbital CN Damage

Traumatic damage to CN III, IV, VI, and V1 — presentations, mechanisms, and pearls.

III

CN III Palsy — Oculomotor

Complete palsy · "Down and out" + ptosis + blown pupil

👁️
Eye Position
"Down and Out"
Unopposed LR (CN VI) + SO (CN IV) → inferolateral deviation
😴
Eyelid
Complete Ptosis
Levator palpebrae superioris lost; eyelid fully dropped
🔦
Pupil
Fixed & Dilated
Parasympathetic lost → sympathetics unopposed → mydriasis; no light reflex
🔭
Diplopia
Present (if eye opened)
Ptosis may mask it. Eye drifts toward midline slowly when looking left (inhibited LR), pops back out fast
🔍
Accommodation
Lost
Ciliary muscle paralyzed → lens cannot thicken → blurred near vision
🚫
Gaze limitation
No elevation, depression, or adduction
Only LR (abduction) and SO (depression/intorsion) remain
🚨 Surgical Emergency
Painful CN III palsy + blown pupil = PComm aneurysm until proven otherwise. Parasympathetics are on the outside of the nerve — aneurysmal compression hits them first → pupil blows before motor signs.

Pupil-sparing CN III palsy = microvascular (DM, HTN) — ischemia hits the core motor axons first, sparing outer parasympathetics. Still gets ptosis + down-and-out.

Why "Down and Out"? — The Vector

With CN III lost, only two muscles remain: Lateral rectus (CN VI) pulls laterally, Superior oblique (CN IV) pulls inferiorly + intorts. Combined vector = inferolateral = "down and out."

IV

CN IV Palsy — Trochlear

Superior oblique palsy · Vertical diplopia · Head tilt · Torticollis

🔄
Eye Position
Extorsion
IO unopposed → 12 o'clock tilts outward; subtle but pathognomonic
👓
Diplopia
Vertical / Oblique
Worst looking down and inward — reading, descending stairs
↩️
Head Tilt
Toward Contralateral Side
Compensatory Bielschowsky sign: tilting to good side intorts it, aligning images
🦴
Neck Pain
Chronic Torticollis
Months of compensatory head tilt → neck strain. Idiopathic unremitting torticollis = CN IV palsy until proven otherwise
👁️
Eyelid
Normal
LPS is CN III — unaffected
💡
Pupil
Normal
No autonomic component in CN IV
🧠 Dr. Willard's Pearl
CN IV has the longest intracranial course of all CNs — wraps all the way around the brainstem. Even a mild indirect head injury (whiplash, concussion) can stretch it. Many patients present with neck pain rather than diplopia (from the compensatory head tilt), making this frequently missed. Idiopathic, unremitting torticollis = trochlear palsy until proven otherwise.
VI

CN VI Palsy — Abducens

Lateral rectus palsy · Esotropia · Horizontal diplopia worse on ipsilateral gaze

👁️
Eye Position
Esotropia (turned in)
MR (CN III) unopposed → eye deviates nasally. Full paralysis: esotropia at rest.
↔️
Diplopia
Horizontal
Side-by-side images. Worse on attempted ipsilateral gaze.
🚫
Abduction
Absent or reduced
Cannot move eye past midline (paralysis) or full abduction lost (paresis)
👁️
Eyelid
Normal
LPS is CN III — intact
💡
Pupil
Normal
No autonomic fibers in CN VI
↕️
Vertical gaze
Preserved
Obliques + SR/IR (CN III + IV) intact
Paralysis vs. Paresis
Complete paralysis: Esotropia at rest + diplopia on forward gaze, worsening on ipsilateral lateral gaze.

Paresis (partial weakness): May maintain conjugate gaze at rest — no diplopia straight ahead. Diplopia appears only when attempting to look to the affected side, worsening as gaze becomes more lateral. (This is what Dr. Willard demonstrated in the clinical video.)
⚠️ False Localizing Sign
Bilateral CN VI palsy from raised ICP: the nerve is stretched over the clivus as the brain herniates downward. The problem is global ICP — not a brainstem lesion. Always consider ICP in bilateral abducens palsy.
V1

V1 Damage — Ophthalmic Division

Purely sensory loss · No motor deficit · Corneal reflex absent

✅
Eye Movements
Normal
V1 is purely sensory — no extraocular muscle involvement
👁️‍🗨️
Corneal Reflex
Absent (afferent limb)
Touch cornea → no blink in either eye. Afferent: nasociliary (V1). Efferent: CN VII. V1 lesion kills the afferent arc.
😶
Forehead
Anesthesia
Supraorbital & supratrochlear nerves (frontal branch V1)
👁️
Upper Eyelid
Anesthesia
Frontal + lacrimal branches of V1
👃
Nasal Tip/Dorsum
Anesthesia
External nasal nerve (nasociliary V1) — Hutchinson's sign territory
💡
Pupil
Normal
No autonomic fibers in V1 — pupillomotor control intact
Herpes Zoster Ophthalmicus — Hutchinson's Sign
VZV reactivation in V1 distribution. Hutchinson's sign = vesicles on the nasal tip (nasociliary branch territory) → strongly predicts ocular involvement (keratitis, uveitis), because the same nasociliary nerve also innervates the cornea and iris. Hutchinson's sign = urgent ophthalmology referral.
Neurotrophic Keratitis
Without V1 sensory input (especially nasociliary), the cornea loses its protective reflex blink and trophic support → neurotrophic keratitis — progressive corneal ulceration → perforation → blindness. Corneal anesthesia is a serious finding.

Comparison Table

Feature CN III CN IV CN VI V1
Eye at restDown & outSlightly elevated, extortedEsotropia (turned in)Normal
Diplopia typeAny direction (hidden by ptosis)Vertical/oblique; worst down & inHorizontal; worst ipsilateral gazeNone (sensory only)
PtosisCompleteNoneNoneNone
PupilDilated fixed (surgical) / Normal (microvascular)NormalNormalNormal
AbductionPreservedPreservedAbsent/reducedNormal
AdductionAbsentPreservedPreservedNormal
Corneal reflexNormalNormalNormalAbsent (afferent)
Head positionNoneTilt to contralateral sideFace turn ipsilateralNone
Classic causePComm aneurysm; uncal herniation; DMHead trauma; congenital; tentorialMicrovascular; raised ICP; clivusOrbital fracture; HZO; cavernous sinus

PRACTICE

15 Orbit & Ocular CN MCQs

High-yield review spanning orbital bones, fissures, annulus relationships, cranial nerve pathways, and classic clinical deficits.

Score 0 / 15 · Answered 0 / 15
Question 1
Unanswered
Which bone forms the largest portion of the orbital floor and therefore contributes most to the classic blowout fracture site?
Answer: B. The maxilla forms most of the orbital floor, which sits above the maxillary sinus and is the classic site of blowout fracture. The palatine contributes only a tiny posterior wedge, and the ethmoid is the thin medial wall.
Question 2
Unanswered
A sinus infection spreads through the thinnest orbital wall into the orbit. Which bony structure was most likely breached?
Answer: D. The lamina papyracea of the ethmoid forms the very thin medial wall of the orbit and lies next to the ethmoid air cells, making it the classic route for spread of sinus infection into the orbit.
Question 3
Unanswered
Which structure passes through the optic canal rather than the superior orbital fissure?
Answer: A. The optic canal transmits CN II and the ophthalmic artery through the lesser wing of the sphenoid. CN III, IV, VI, and V1 branches enter through the superior orbital fissure instead.
Question 4
Unanswered
Which nerve runs through the cavernous sinus directly on the internal carotid artery instead of in the lateral wall?
Answer: C. CN VI is the exception: it courses medially within the cavernous sinus, closely related to the internal carotid artery. CN III, IV, and V1 are arranged in the lateral wall.
Question 5
Unanswered
Which structure enters the orbit through the superior orbital fissure but remains outside and above the annulus of Zinn?
Answer: B. CN IV passes through the superior orbital fissure above and outside the annulus of Zinn. In contrast, CN III divisions, CN VI, and the nasociliary nerve pass through the ring.
Question 6
Unanswered
A patient has complete ptosis, a fixed dilated pupil, and an eye resting inferolaterally. Which lesion best explains this pattern?
Answer: A. CN III palsy causes ptosis from levator loss, mydriasis from parasympathetic loss, and the classic "down and out" eye due to unopposed CN VI and CN IV action.
Question 7
Unanswered
Which clinical scenario most strongly suggests a compressive aneurysm rather than a microvascular ischemic lesion of CN III?
Answer: D. Painful CN III palsy with pupillary involvement is the classic red flag for a posterior communicating artery aneurysm. Microvascular ischemic palsies tend to spare the pupil.
Question 8
Unanswered
A patient reports vertical diplopia that is worst while reading and walking downstairs, and she habitually tilts her head to the opposite side. Which nerve is most likely affected?
Answer: C. CN IV palsy weakens superior oblique, so symptoms are worst on down-and-in gaze such as reading or descending stairs. Patients often compensate with a contralateral head tilt.
Question 9
Unanswered
A patient cannot abduct the right eye and develops horizontal diplopia that worsens when looking right. Which muscle has lost its innervation?
Answer: B. Failure of abduction with horizontal diplopia on ipsilateral gaze is the signature of CN VI palsy affecting the lateral rectus muscle.
Question 10
Unanswered
Touching the right cornea produces no blink in either eye, but eye movements and pupils are normal. Which damaged pathway best explains this finding?
Answer: D. The corneal reflex uses V1, especially the nasociliary branch, as the afferent limb and CN VII as the efferent limb. Loss of the right V1 afferent prevents blinking in either eye when that cornea is touched.
Question 11
Unanswered
Vesicles on the tip of the nose in herpes zoster ophthalmicus are especially concerning because they indicate involvement of which sensory branch?
Answer: A. Hutchinson's sign refers to vesicles on the nasal tip, indicating nasociliary involvement. That matters because the nasociliary nerve also supplies the cornea, increasing the risk of ocular complications.
Question 12
Unanswered
Which orbital structure receives preganglionic parasympathetic fibers from the inferior division of CN III before sending short ciliary nerves to the eye?
Answer: C. Preganglionic parasympathetic fibers travel in the inferior division of CN III to the ciliary ganglion. Postganglionic fibers then travel through the short ciliary nerves to the sphincter pupillae and ciliary muscle.
Question 13
Unanswered
Which combination of structures passes through the common tendinous ring at the orbital apex?
Answer: B. The annulus of Zinn encloses the optic canal and part of the superior orbital fissure, transmitting CN II, both divisions of CN III, CN VI, and the nasociliary nerve. CN IV, frontal, and lacrimal pass above it.
Question 14
Unanswered
Which finding would be most consistent with bilateral abducens palsy acting as a false localizing sign from raised intracranial pressure?
Answer: D. Raised ICP can stretch CN VI over the clivus, creating bilateral abducens palsy without a focal brainstem lesion. That is why bilateral abduction weakness is considered a false localizing sign.
Question 15
Unanswered
A lesion at the superior orbital fissure causes ophthalmoplegia and loss of forehead sensation, but vision remains intact. Which explanation best fits this presentation?
Answer: A. The superior orbital fissure carries CN III, IV, VI, and V1, so lesions there cause ophthalmoplegia plus V1 sensory loss. Vision can remain intact because CN II and the ophthalmic artery travel through the optic canal instead.
